ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Bún riêu cua. The closest defensible FoodOn anchor is the generic noodle soup food product class. A Vietnamese crab noodle soup-specific term is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Vietnamese crab noodle soup. A0CKP (Soups and broths) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to Bún riêu cua specifically. SNOMED CT coverage of Vietnamese regional dishes is absent.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Bún riêu cua weighs approximately 600 g and contains rice vermicelli noodles, crab-tomato broth, crab paste, minced pork, tofu, blood cubes, and fresh garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Use in East Asia and Southeast Asia — Vietnamese composite noodle soup reference; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central component data for rice vermicelli, crab, pork, tofu, and tomato
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Bún riêu cua as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Vietnamese noodle soups and USDA FoodData Central component data for rice vermicelli (FDC ID 168871), blue crab (FDC ID 174202), ground pork (FDC ID 168351), firm tofu (FDC ID 172475), and tomatoes (FDC ID 170457), weighted to a representative recipe composition. Input per_100g values (energy 91.67 kcal, protein 5.83 g, fat 4.17 g, carbohydrate 7.5 g) are consistent with this component-weighted estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 300 mg/100g is plausible given fermented condiment load (fish sauce, shrimp paste, crab paste).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice vermicelli (boiled) GI reference; composite dish GL estimated from carbohydrate content per serving
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bún riêu cua as a composite dish. GI of 53 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source: boiled rice vermicelli (GI ~53, University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2021, Diabetes Care). The broth, protein, fat, and vegetable components lower the effective GI relative to plain rice vermicelli. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (53 × 45.0 g) / 100 = 23.85, rounded to 23.9. Available carbohydrate per 600 g serving = 7.5 g/100g × 600 g = 45.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium load from fermented condiments (fish sauce, shrimp paste, crab paste) contributing approximately 1800 mg sodium per standard 600 g bowl
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and shrimp paste in the broth, avoid adding condiments at the table, and limit portion size to reduce total sodium intake below 600 mg per meal where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined rice vermicelli noodles with low dietary fibre (0.67 g/100g) drive moderate postprandial glycaemic response despite moderate overall carbohydrate content (7.5 g/100g); estimated GI 53 and GL 23.9 per bowl
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should request a smaller noodle portion, increase the proportion of tofu, bean sprouts, and vegetables to moderate glycaemic impact, and avoid adding sweet condiments or extra sugar to the broth.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (300 mg/100g; ~1800 mg per bowl) and cholesterol (30 mg/100g; ~180 mg per bowl) from crab, pork, and blood cubes, with saturated fat at 1.33 g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, request reduced fermented condiments to lower sodium, and moderate intake of pork and blood cubes to manage saturated fat and dietary cholesterol within daily cardiovascular risk targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Minced pork (red meat high in arachidonic acid) and nightshade vegetables (tomatoes, chilli) may amplify inflammatory pathways in RA-sensitive individuals; high sodium from fermented condiments worsens tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should consider requesting a pork-free version with extra tofu or crab, monitor for joint flare responses to tomatoes and chilli, and request reduced fish sauce and shrimp paste to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bún riêu cua (Vietnamese Crab Noodle Soup) is a traditional Vietnamese noodle soup dish built on a rich, tangy tomato-based broth flavoured with crab paste (mắm cua), fish sauce, and shrimp paste. The soup is served over thin rice vermicelli noodles (bún) and typically includes minced pork or pork ribs, firm tofu, pork blood cubes (tiết), and a garnish of fresh herbs such as perilla, Vietnamese balm, and bean sprouts. The broth is characterised by its deep umami depth from fermented seafood condiments balanced by the acidity of ripe tomatoes and the sweetness of fresh crab. The dish is boiled and simmered, resulting in a savoury, slightly tangy, and aromatic soup with a soft noodle texture and tender protein components. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 91.7 kcal, 5.8 g protein, 4.2 g fat, and 7.5 g carbohydrate, with a notable sodium content of 300 mg/100g driven by multiple fermented condiments. The estimated glycemic index of 53 (low-medium range) reflects the rice vermicelli base, moderated by the protein, fat, and vegetable components. Clinically, the high cumulative sodium per bowl (~1800 mg) is the primary concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The dish is not suitable for strict halal diets due to pork and blood cube content, and contains crustacean, shellfish, soy, and fish allergens. Bún riêu cua is widely consumed across Vietnam as a breakfast or lunch dish and is available in Vietnamese restaurants throughout Southeast Asia, including Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Bún riêu cua as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA FoodData Central component data for the key ingredients (rice vermicelli, crab, pork, tofu, tomato), weighted to a representative recipe. The input per_100g values are consistent with this component-weighted estimate and are retained. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source (boiled rice vermicelli, GI 53) per the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence reflects the absence of a direct dish-level measurement in any approved source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite Vietnamese crab noodle soup dish are not available as a single entry in FAO/INFOODS SEA, USDA FoodData Central, or Singapore HPB databases as of April 2026. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.02 g/100g from the input record, consistent with trace levels from pork fat; this is a plausible trace value. All core macros (protein_g 5.83, fat_g 4.17, carbohydrate_g 7.5) are non-zero and consistent with a mixed crab-pork-noodle soup dish; no zero-macro justification is required. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on boiled rice vermicelli as the dominant carbohydrate source (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2021). The input listed country_of_origin as Singapore, which likely reflects the data collection context (GrabFood Singapore); the dish's true culinary origin is Vietnam and this is reflected in region_of_origin and cuisine fields. The input source listed FAO/INFOODS as supporting only menu_listing; nutrient values from the input are treated as estimated and cross-validated against component data.
